coredata.dc:title: NH2-rich Carbon Quantum Dots: A protein-responsive probe for detection and identification
coredata.dc:creator: Freire, R.M.
Le, Ngoc D.B.
Jiang, Ziwen
Kim, Chang Soo
Rotello, Vincent M.
Fechine, P.B.A.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we report the synthesis and application of polyethylenimine, ethylenediamine branched-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs.BPEI) for protein sensing. These carbon-based nanoparticles were found to act as a protein-responsive platform. On this basis, the CQDs.BPEI system was able to detect eight different proteins (four metallic and four non-metallic), even using concentrations in the range of 5–40nM, depending on the analyte protein. The fluorescence titrations performed at 298 and 310K displayed fluorescence quenching, which can be explained through a collisional mechanism. It was also possible to conclude that the fluorescence quench comes from the amino acid residues on the surface of the proteins. To further check the potential of the system, we developed a “nose”-based methodology to identify proteins. Using materials, such as copper acetate and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, our chemical “nose” approach was able to discriminate 40nM proteins in phosphate buffered saline (pH 7.4). In this case, the identification accuracy of the random unknown set was 83%. Overall, the results evidence the potential of CQDs.BPEI as a protein-responsive probe to detect and identify proteins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence
Proteins
Detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Science Without Borders program ( Capes- Brazil, BEX 13936/13-1 ); and the National Institutes of Health ( GM077173 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and surface properties of CQDs.BPEI. (a) TEM image of CQDs.BPEI, The dashed red circle indicates an area where NPs can be seen. inset: distribution size graph based on the measurements of 150 particles randomly chosen. (b) FT-IR spectrum of CQDs.BPEI. XPS analysis: (c) survey scan, (d) C1s analysis, (e) N1s analysis and (f) illustration of the expected CQDs.BPEI surface structure. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Optical properties of CQDs.BPEI. (a) UV/Vis absorption spectra of CQDs.BPEI. (b) Excitation wavelength independent fluorescence spectra of CQDs.BPEI. (c) Fluorescence intensity as a function of pH values. (d) Photobleaching experiment with fluorescence intensity recorded for 60min at room temperature.

Fluorescence titration performed at 298 and 310K for non-metallic (a) and metallic (b) proteins. Fluorescence intensity was investigated at 445nm using an excitation wavelength of 350nm and normalized using
I 1
I 0
, where I 0 and I 1 denotes the intensity before and after protein addition, respectively. Each average value and standard deviation was calculated based on three replicates.

Detection of eight different proteins in PBS. (a) Schematic picture illustrating the “nose”-based strategy developed. (b) Fluorescence response pattern of the analyte proteins. Each average value and standard deviation was calculated based on six replicates. The fluorescence response for each bar in the graph is normalized using
I n
I 0
, where n denotes the binding event. (c) Canonical score plot for the fluorescence patterns obtained from LDA utilizing the two most significant factors.

head.ce:title: NH2-rich Carbon Quantum Dots: A protein-responsive probe for detection and identification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Grupo de Química de Materiais Avançados (GQMAT)- Departamento de Química Analítica e Físico-Química, Universidade Federal do Ceará − UFC, Campus do Pici, CP 12100, CEP 60451-970 Fortaleza, CE, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Grupo de Química de Materiais Avançados (GQMAT)- Departamento de Química Analítica e Físico-Química

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Massachusetts,710 North Pleasant Street, Amherst, MA 01003, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we report the synthesis and application of polyethylenimine, ethylenediamine branched-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs.BPEI) for protein sensing. These carbon-based nanoparticles were found to act as a protein-responsive platform. On this basis, the CQDs.BPEI system was able to detect eight different proteins (four metallic and four non-metallic), even using concentrations in the range of 5–40nM, depending on the analyte protein. The fluorescence titrations performed at 298 and 310K displayed fluorescence quenching, which can be explained through a collisional mechanism. It was also possible to conclude that the fluorescence quench comes from the amino acid residues on the surface of the proteins. To further check the potential of the system, we developed a “nose”-based methodology to identify proteins. Using materials, such as copper acetate and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, our chemical “nose” approach was able to discriminate 40nM proteins in phosphate buffered saline (pH 7.4). In this case, the identification accuracy of the random unknown set was 83%. Overall, the results evidence the potential of CQDs.BPEI as a protein-responsive probe to detect and identify proteins.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials. These NPs have been gaining significant attention due to their excellent properties [1], since their accidental discovery by Xu et al. [2] in 2004. Good solubility, high quantum yield and resistance to photobleaching are some of these characteristics [3]. Relevant biological properties, such as low toxicity and biocompatibility, can also be mentioned [4]. Therefore, CQDs have been exploited by many researchers worldwide. As a consequence, a wide range of applications, including sensing [5], imaging [6], nanomedicine [7] and photocatalysis [8], have emerged. However, it is important to highlight the application of CQDs for sensing purposes. By exploiting their fluorescent properties, a broad variety of different analytes, like heavy metals [9,10], organic pollutants [11] and drugs [12], can be detected.

In recent years, CQDs have been investigated to sense molecules inside biological systems (e.g. cells [13]) in order to detect diseases in the early stages, as this would increase the chance of curing patients. In this regard, the detection of proteins is very important as these biomolecules can serve as biomarkers related to diseases, such as cancer [14]. Thus, real-time analytical protein detection is strongly desired to facilitate medical diagnosis. Some approaches to analyze proteins are based on high-resolution two-dimensional polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis, mass spectroscopy and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) [15,16]. Among these methods, the most used format to obtain clinical protein determinations is ELISA [17]. In this system, the proteins are detected through a “lock–key” approach. Therefore, receptors of high affinity and specificity immobilized onto surfaces bind the analyte proteins. Afterwards, a secondary receptor is combined to react with chromogenic or fluorogenic substrates to generate detectable signals [18,19]. The limit of detection for this classical sensing protocol is ∼1pgml−1 (lower limit of reliable quantification), which evidences its high sensitivity. Although, the method is time-consuming requiring specific and expensive receptors (high production cost), as well as instrumentation and expertise for optimal use. This restricts its applicability.

Despite significant efforts, new strategies to monitor protein levels remain a serious issue in medical diagnosis. In this context, some publications have demonstrated that proteins can be detected using CQDs [20–23]. However, the full potential of CQDs for protein sensing is still unknown. Therefore, a better understanding of the interaction between CQDs and protein, as well as being able to modulate them, is essential to perform the detection approach. Furthermore, protein identification must also be carried out in order to get a more robust sensing strategy. To do this, the chemical “nose/tongue” approach provides an alternative totally opposed to “lock–key” specific protein recognition. Since a sensor array formed by different receptors is able to differentially respond against distinct analytes [19]. Over the past few years, this differential strategy has been used to sense a variety of analytes, such as proteins [24], cells [25], bacteria [26] and other [27–30]. In general, the reports exploit multiples NPs to obtain multiple interactional events, which are used to provide discrimination. For example, Edward et al. [31] have been designed a sensor array based on eight gold fluorescent nanodots having different surface properties. Each nanodot was used to get one binding event and the authors were capable to successfully distinguish 8 different proteins, affording 100% accuracy for the unknowns. However, Rotello et al. [32] recently demonstrated a host-guest sensing approach to double the number of output channels. The authors exploited the interaction between cucurbit [7] uril and gold NPs to modify the binding of three fluorescent proteins of a multi-channel sensor in situ. As a consequence, the sensor became more reliable and robust. By using this sensing platform, the sensor was able to readily differentiate the complex cell lysates (100% accuracy). Thus, the modulation of the binding event can be a versatile tool to easily increase the information content of an array-based sensor.

Given the exposed, our current study reports the synthesis and application of NH2-rich CQDs as a protein-responsive probe. On this basis, eight different proteins (four metallic and four non-metallic) were evaluated against the fluorescence intensity of a CQDs.BPEI system. A chemical “nose” approach to identify proteins combining CQDs, Cu2+ and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) was also developed. Using these components, the “nose”-based strategy was able to generate characteristic fluorescence patterns with high accuracy in PBS. Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) was further used to successfully ascribe the fluorescence patterns, affording 98% accuracy according to the Jackknife classification. This demonstrates the excellent ability of the CQDs.BPEI for protein detection and discrimination.

The chemicals used in this study were citric acid anhydrous (C6H8O7, 99.5%, Aldrich), polyethylenimine, ethylenediamine branched (BPEI) (H(NHCH2CH2)nNH2, average Mw of 800, Aldrich), sodium phosphate monobasic (NaH2PO4·H2O, Fisher), dibasic (Na2HPO4), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.0–100.5%, Mallinckrodt), calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2, 96.0%, Fisher), cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2 4H2O, 99.0%, Fluka), cobalt (II) chloride (CoCl2·6H2O, Fluka), copper acetate (Cu(OOCCH3)2·H2O, 98.0%, Alfa Aesar), iron (II) chloride (FeCl2·xH2O, 99.0%, Alfa Aesar), manganese chloride (MnCl2·4H2O, Fisher), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, Fisher), iron (III) nitrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 99.9%, Acros Organics), EDTA (C10H16N2O8, Alfa Aesar) and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4, Invitrogen). The analyte proteins, bovine serum albumin (BSA), human serum albumin (HSA), acid phosphatase (PhosA, from potato) and alkaline phosphatase (PhosB, from bovine intestinal mucosa), hemoglobin (Hem, from human), myoglobin (Myo, from equine heart), α-amylase (α-Am, from Bacillus licheniformis) and cytochrome c (Cyt.C, from equine heart), were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received. A dialysis membrane tubing cut-off in the range of 0.5–1.0kDa was supplied by Spectrum Laboratories.

The CQDs.BPEI system was synthesized according to a known procedure [33]. However, some modifications were applied in order to perform the capping using BPEI. Briefly, 2g of citric acid anhydrous were weighted and transferred to a 20mL glass vial. Then, 1g of BPEI was added and the mixture was heated at 180°C for 30min. After cooling down, mili-Q water (resistivity of 18.2MΩ·cm) was added to extract the CQDs.BPEI and the final solution was purified through dialysis membrane for 24h. Finally, the solution was lyophilized to obtain the powder. It was kept in the fridge at 4°C for further application and characterization.

The absorbance and fluorescent measurements were recorded using a fluorescence microplate reader (Molecular Devices SpectraMax M5). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out on an ALPHA FT-IR spectrometer in the range of 4000–400cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on a Physical Electronics Quantum 2000 spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα excitation at a spot size of 10mm with a pass energy of 46.95eV. To confirm the size and shape of CQDs.BPEI, transmission electron microscope (TEM) samples were prepared using a drop-casting method. Briefly, a drop of the CQD solution (5μL) was placed onto a 300-mesh nickel grid coated with Formvar film and allowed to dry overnight at room temperature. The sample was then measured using a JEOL 200FX TEM. The concentration of CQDs.BPEI in solution was obtained by building up a calibration curve (concentration versus absorbance). The photobleaching resistance was also investigated. In this regard, 200μL of the CQDs.BPEI solution (2μg.mL−1) was firstly loaded into a well on a 96-well plate (300mL Whatman black bottom microplate). Then, the plate was placed in the microplate reader and the fluorescence intensity at 445nm was sequentially recorded (excitation at 350nm) during 60min. Between measurements, the laser irradiation was stopped and the 96-well plate was kept inside the microplate reader.

Initially, a stock solution of CQDs.BPEI was prepared in phosphate buffer (PB, 5mM, pH 7.4). The absorbance of this solution at 350nm was measured and the concentration of CQDs was precisely obtained based on the calibration curve. Afterwards, a stock solution of each analyte protein was also prepared for the sensing experiments. The concentrations of these solutions were acquired by absorbance measurements at 280nm. Unlike CQDs.BPEI, the concentration of proteins was calculated using the Beer-Lambert law (
C =
A
280
/
(
ε
280
⋅ l
)
). For the initial detection experiments, 200μL of the CQDs.BPEI solution (2μg.mL−1) was loaded into a well on a 96-well plate (300mL Whatman black bottom microplate) and the fluorescence intensity value at 445nm was measured using excitation at 350nm. This value was considered the initial intensity (I 0). Subsequently, 10μL of protein solution with an adequate concentration was added in order to obtain 40nM in the well. After 1min, the fluorescence intensity was recorded again (I). The value found for the
I
I 0
ratio was the investigated response. For all proteins, the procedure was the same and the process was repeated three times. For the fluorescence titration studies, the protein concentration in the well was adjusted to obtain values from 5 to 40nM. The same procedure described above was applied to obtain the fluorescence response. Therefore, the fluorescence response was recorded as a function of the analyte protein concentrations. In order to further investigate the fluorescence quenching, the titration experiments were performed at 298 and 310K. All experiments described up to this point here were carried out in PB.

For the development of the “nose”-based methodology used in this work, first, it was necessary to prepare stock solutions of all metallic cations. On this basis, stock solutions of appropriate concentrations were prepared in order to obtain 250μM in the well. Then, the fluorescence response was recorded before (I 0) and upon (I) addition of 10μL (three replicates). A careful analysis of the fluorescence response (
I
I 0
) was performed and the metal ion displaying the best quenching efficiency was chosen to compose the strategy. A titration study with this metal was also required. Thus, the same procedure described for fluorescence titrations was applied. Afterwards, an EDTA stock solution was prepared. The desired concentration in the well for this chemical was 1mM. After setting up all the conditions, the methodology was tested in PBS. In this regard, the CQDs.BPEI stock solution, previously prepared in PB, was diluted using PBS buffer to afford the final concentration of 2μg.mL−1. Then, 200μL of this diluted solution was placed into a well on a 96-well plate. Using excitation at 350nm, the fluorescence intensity at 445nm was recorded (I 0). Subsequently, 10μL of protein solution was added (40nM in the well) and the fluorescence intensity was acquired again (I 1) after 1min. The ratio
I 1
I 0
was the first response. Next, 10μL of the solution containing the chosen metal ion was added to each well and the fluorescence intensity value at 445nm was obtained (I 2). Therefore, the ratio
I 2
I 0
was considered the second response. Finally, 10μL of EDTA solution was added into the well (final concentration of 1mM). The fluorescence intensity was recorded again (I 3) and the ratio
I 3
I 0
was interpreted as the third response. This process was repeated for all analyte proteins six times, which generates a three binding events x eight proteins x six replicates training data matrix. The full raw data set was then processed using LDA in SYSTAT (version 11.0). A set of random unknown samples (48 samples) was identified using a similar procedure.

Fig. 1 (a) shows the TEM micrograph, as well as the distribution size graph (inset) of CQDs.BPEI. This graph was built by measuring 150 particles randomly chosen. The average NP size was estimated using a normal distribution. The micrograph evidences CQDs.BPEI with good uniformity and monodispersity, since a unimodal distribution and no agglomerates can be observed, respectively. The value for average NP size was found to be 1.45±0.34nm. Similar results can be observed in the literature [34–36]. In order to identify the surface chemistry (functional groups and elemental states) of the CQDs.BPEI, FT-IR and XPS were performed. The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs.BPEI recorded at room temperature is displayed in Fig. 1(b). Absorption bands arising from carboxyl (CO/COOH) and hydroxyl (OH) groups were observed at 1702 and 1378cm−1, respectively. In the range of 1700–2000cm−1, weak bands attributed to the substitution pattern of the benzene ring can be seen. The absorption bands from N were also observed. Clearly, there is a broad band above 3000cm−1, which can be assigned to the NH stretching of primary amines. At around 1600cm−1, NH bending bands from primary and secondary amines can be observed. Furthermore, CN stretching bands from aliphatic amines were also observed in the range of 1200–1020cm−1.

Fig. 1(c) shows the XPS survey scan of the studied system. Clearly, the spectrum reveals peaks at 286, 399, 530 and 687eV, which can be attributed to the C, N, O and F species, respectively [37,38]. This result evidences the N content on the surface of the CQDs. Therefore, the capping performed was confirmed. Alternatively, an unexpected peak related to the F displayed the presence of the impurities. In order to further our study, the detailed spectra for C and N are shown in Fig. 1(d) and (e), respectively. In Fig. 1(d), five different components were identified. The C1s spectrum displays predominant sp2 and sp3 graphitic C peaks at 284.2 (CC) and 284.7 (CC) eV, respectively [39,40]. It was also possible to see peaks from CN, COC and CO at 286.0, 286.6 and 286.8eV, respectively [41]. Once again, the N content was confirmed on the surface. In addition, the other observed groups evidence the presence of oxygen on the surface, which is plausible since citric acid was used as a carbon source to synthesize the studied CQDs. Fig. 1(e) shows the N1s spectrum where four components can be found. The predominant intensities were observed for the peaks assigned to the pyridinic and amine (-NH2) species at 397.3 and 399.1eV, respectively [42–44]. Furthermore, a comparative study of these peaks revealed a large amount of NH2 groups on the surface, since the peak at 399.1eV showed the highest intensity. Highly substituted amine groups and NOx species were also evidenced at 402.0 and 404.0eV, respectively [45]. The results from XPS and FT-IR are in good agreement.

In order to investigate the optical properties of the CQDs.BPEI, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed. The UV–vis spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2 (a). A typical band centered at 230nm can be found. According to the literature, this band is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [46]. A band at ca. 270nm was also observed, which denotes the nπ* transition coming from the CO bond [47]. Another strong and symmetrical absorption band at ca. 350nm was observed. This notable band is attributed to the nπ* transition, as well as contributions of the surface groups. Furthermore, the NH2 group is expected to be electron-donating, which may improve the functionalization degree and electron transitions. This explains the very strong and symmetrical absorption band evidencing the high amount of NH2 groups on the surfaces [48]. In agreement with this discussion, the emission should also be affected by the passivation. In this regard, Fig. 2(b) presents fluorescent spectra recorded for CQDs.BPEI at different excitation wavelengths. Clearly, the CQDs synthesized in this work were found to be excitation independent. The emission bands were no longer shifted by changing the excitation wavelength. Therefore, an absence of surface states between the HOMO and LUMO can be inferred, which leads to a single transition mode. Considering that NH2 groups have a contribution for the electron structure of CQDs.BPEI, this behavior confirms the large amounts of these species on the surface and indicates good homogeneity of the passivation process. In addition, Fig. 2(c) and (d) present the fluorescence intensity as a function of pH and time, respectively. The intensity was statically constant in a solution of pH range 5–9. However, high or low values of pH decreased the intensity. Minor (∼5%) photobleaching was observed during the 60min.

In order to investigate the potential of the CQDs.BPEI as a protein-responsive probe, eight different proteins were tested. In this regard, four metallic (Hem, Myo, α-Am and Cyt.C) and four non-metallic (BSA, HSA, PhosA and PhosB) proteins were chosen. Table S1 displays some basic properties of the analyte proteins. Furthermore, Fig. S1 presents the fluorescence response (
I
I 0
) of the CQDs.BPEI upon addition of the proteins. Clearly, except by Cyt.C, the evaluated proteins were capable of quenching the fluorescence intensity, even using a concentration as low as 40nM. This evidences the high potential of the CQDs.BPEI as a protein-responsive sensor.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) display the titrations at 298 and 310K for non-metallic and metallic proteins, respectively. By observing the plots (
I
I 0
vs [P]) at 298K, it was possible to see a deviation of linearity at high protein concentration. This indicates the presence of both collisional (dynamic) and binding-related (static) quenching mechanisms [49]. However, in order to discriminate between one another, the titrations were also performed at 310K. As a consequence of the increase of temperature, the quenching efficiency was higher for all evaluated proteins. According to the literature [50], the increase of the temperature should increase the quenching efficiency for the collisional mechanism, while the opposite should be expected for binding-related quenching. Therefore, the results in Fig. 3(a) and (b) can be explained by the increase of collisions with increasing temperature, which leads to higher quenching efficiency.

Beyond detection, a probe should also identify the analyte [30,31,51–54]. On this basis, a strategy based on a chemical nose/tongue approach [55–57] can be used to perform the identification of proteins. This is further supported by looking at the fluorescence response observed in Fig. 3(a), since each evaluated protein had a unique response, which may indicate the possibility of direct transduction. Therefore, the first receptor was the CQDs.BPEI itself. However, it is well-known that a “nose”-based methodology must provide multiple binding events to build a recognition pattern for each analyte. Most of the discrimination approaches applied includes NPs having different surface properties [24,58–61]. In this case, a distinct pattern of responses is generated by the interaction between different NPs and the target analyte. Although, the NPs surface engineer of the NPs used as a sensing platform can make the process more expensive, which is undesired.

Thus, in order to obtain multivalent interactions in this work, another two sequential steps were added to generate extra differentiation instead of use different CQDs. Although, before completing the additional steps, it is worth mentioning the use of CQDs as a fluorescent probe for metal ions [10]. Aligned to this, proteins can also interact with these entities [62–64] and the metal-binding site interaction is affected by the matrix in different ways [65]. Taken together, metallic cations seem to be a potential tool to compose a “nose”-based strategy. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs.BPEI was firstly evaluated against 250μM of various metallic cations (K+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Zn2+ and Fe3+). The results are displayed in Fig. S2. Clearly, the best quenching efficiency was obtained using Cu2+. In recent years, some publications [66,67] involving NH2-rich CQDs demonstrated their significant potential for Cu2+ detection even for pH values above 7 [66]. Additionally, work recently published presented the stability of Cu2+ in different buffer solutions including PBS [68]. No Cu-related precipitates were observed until the pH reached 8. Once the sensing experiments were carried out at pH 7.4, Cu2+ was the metallic cation selected to obtain a second differential binding event. In this regard, the concentration of Cu2+ to be used was investigated through fluorescence titration presented in Fig. S3. Based on this experiment, 1μM of Cu2+ was the concentration chosen, since the fluorescence intensity of CQDs.BPEI may decrease or increase depending on the binding affinity of the Cu2+ towards CQDs.BPEI or analyte protein.

Once a metallic cation was chosen to build a “nose”-based strategy, another multivalent interaction can be obtained using a chelating agent. In this regard, EDTA can provide a selective binding with Cu2+ forming a negatively charged complex ([Cu-EDTA]2−). As a consequence, higher fluorescence intensity would be observed, since the formation of the mentioned complex implies the release of Cu2+ from the surface of the CQDs.BPEI. However, the interaction between Cu2+ and the CQDs.BPEI-protein system can disturb this removal process. Thus, unique fluorescence intensity would be expected for each protein, which makes the third discriminant event of the “nose”-based strategy. In addition, the literature [69,70] reports EDTA can also interact with proteins. This may further contribute to improve the differentiation of the third binding event. Fig. 4 (a) presents an illustration representing the modulation of the fluorescence response by the competitive binding between CQDs.BPEI, proteins, Cu2+ and EDTA.

Once the methodology was developed, it was used to investigate the ability to identify proteins (40nM) in PBS using 2μg.mL−1, 1μM and 1mM of CQDs.BPEI, Cu2+ and EDTA, respectively. Fig. 4(b) displays the fluorescence response of the multiples interactional events. These recognition patterns were found to be reproducible and characteristic for each protein. In order to further the pattern analysis, LDA (supervised statistical method) was employed as a chemometric tool to maximize the differentiation of the fluorescence response [71]. By analyzing the full set of data (three binding events x eight proteins x six replicates, Table S2), two canonical factors (90.1 and 9.8%) were generated. These two were used to build up the LDA plot, as can be seen in Fig. 4(c). The “nose”-based strategy developed in this work can successfully discriminate the analyte proteins. According to the jackknife matrix, 98% of analyte proteins were correctly classified (Table S3). These results evidence the ability of CQDs.BPEI toward protein differentiation. Furthermore, the identification efficiency was validated using a random set of unknowns containing seven different proteins chosen from a training set (three binding events x seven proteins x six replicates, Table S4). We observed an identification accuracy of 83% (40 out of 48 samples) for the randomized set. Most of the misclassified cases were found to be between BSA and HSA.

We have presented the NH2-rich CQDs synthesized in this work as a protein-responsive system. Using both metallic and non-metallic proteins, CQDs.BPEI displayed the ability to detect concentrations as low as 5nM depending of the evaluated protein. This evidences the potential of CQDs.BPEI as a potential protein-responsive platform to perform the detection. Significantly, the sensor has the ability to discriminate different proteins, since unique fluorescence response was observed, which allows a direct transduction. Furthermore, by combining materials, like copper ions and EDTA, with CQDs.BPEI, we were able to develop a fast “nose”-based strategy to differentiate proteins. The approach successfully identified 40 samples of the random unknown set (48 samples) in PBS affording 83% accuracy. Taken together, the results illustrate CQDs.BPEI to be a protein-responsive platform.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science Without Borders program (Capes- Brazil, BEX 13936/13-1); and the National Institutes of Health (GM077173).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.09.085.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rafael M. Freire received his master degree (Inorganic chemistry) from Federal University of Ceará (Brazil) in 2012. Next, he earned his doctor degree in 2016 at the same institution. Currently, he is a postdoctoral in Physics Department of the Federal University of Ceará. His research focuses in magnetic materials and protein sensing using carbon quantum dots as a probe.

Ngoc D. B. Le received her BS (summa cum laude) from Shorter University, GA in 2011. Currently, she is a PhD candidate in the Department of Chemistry at the University of Massachusetts, Amherst under the guidance of Professor Vincent M. Rotello. Her research focuses on developing high-throughput screening assays for different drug and environmental toxin profiling.

Ziwen Jiang received his BS in Chemistry from Nanjing University, China in 2012, with University Honors. Currently, he is a graduate student in the Department of Chemistry at the University of Massachusetts. His current research focuses on stimuli-responsive materials.

Chang Soo Kim received his BS in Chemical Engineering from Sung Kyun Kwan University in Seoul, South Korea. Next, he earned his MS in Engineering with a concentration in Chemical Engineering from California State University in Long Beach. His PhD is from the University of California, Irvine, in Chemical Engineering and Biochemical Engineering. Currently, he is a research scientist at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center. His research focuses on developing nanotechnology for the early stage of cancer detection.

Vincent Rotello is Goessmann Professor of Chemistry and University Distinguished Professor at the University of Massachusetts–Amherst. He joined the University of Massachusetts in 1993, receiving the NSF CAREER, Cottrell Scholar and Camille Dreyfus TeacherScholar awards, the Sloan Fellowship, and the Langmuir Lectureship, and is a Fellow of the AAAS and the Royal Society of Chemistry (UK). He is the Editor-in-Chief of Bioconjugate Chemistry, and on the Editorial Board of 12 other journals. His research program focuses on engineering the interface between hard and soft materials for applications in nanotechnology and nanomedicine, with >430 papers published to date.

Pierre B. A. Fechine is a professor in Analytical Chemistry and Physical Chemistry Department, Federal University of Ceará (Brazil). He received his master degree (Inorganic Chemistry) and doctor degree (Inorganic Chemistry) from Federal University of Ceará (Brazil) in 2004 and in 2008, respectively. His current research interests include Materials chemistry, condensed state physics, dielectric and magnetic properties, biomaterials and magnetic nanoparticles.
